<!-- DRAFT section to insert into drmTMB vignettes/julia-engine.Rmd, after the
     bivariate Gaussian section / "What the bridge returns". Mirrors the rev5
     Ayumi reply. Numbers are the verified output of
     report/finish-audit/ayumi-profile-bootstrap-via-R.R (R -> Julia -> R).
     HOLD committing to drmTMB until DRM.jl #287/#286/#19 are merge-ready, and
     keep this caveated as the direct-JuliaCall path (the bridge gates bivariate
     phylo). -->

## Boundary-honest among-axis SD intervals for the bivariate phylogenetic model

The bivariate `phylo(1 | species)` location-scale model (`mu1`, `mu2`, `sigma1`,
`sigma2`, `rho12`) estimates a 4x4 among-axis covariance `Sigma_a`, whose
diagonal square roots are the among-species SDs on the two means and the two
log-scales. On real data a *scale* axis often carries no detectable
phylogenetic signal: its SD collapses toward 0. At that boundary the Hessian of
the negative log-likelihood is **singular** -- a variance component sitting at 0
has no curvature in its direction -- so the observed-information matrix cannot
be inverted and the native fit reports `pdHess = FALSE`: the Wald standard
errors and `vcov` come back `NaN`. This is structural, not a numerical glitch;
a finer optimiser cannot manufacture curvature that is not there.

The fix is an interval that does not need the Hessian. DRM.jl provides two,
reached from R via JuliaCall (the `engine = "julia"` bridge routes *bivariate
phylogenetic* fits to native `engine = "tmb"` for now -- a deliberate gate
pending parity tests -- so the bivariate among-axis intervals go through the
direct call; idiomatic `confint()` wiring is tracked):

* **`profile_sigma_a(fit)` -- profile-likelihood CIs (calibrated, the one to
  reach for first).** For each axis SD it fixes that SD on a grid, re-optimises
  every other parameter, and inverts the likelihood ratio. No Hessian; and
  because SDs are bounded below by 0, a collapsed axis returns a lower bound of
  **exactly 0** -- the honest "no detectable scale-phylo signal" interval.
* **`bootstrap_sigma_a(fit; data, B)` -- parametric bootstrap (detection +
  correlations).** Percentile interval per axis SD, plus the six among-axis
  coevolution correlations each with a CI -- which the SD-by-SD profile does not
  give. Use it for the magnitude read and the correlation question. Its
  scale-axis interval *width* is not calibrated (it inherits the ML shrinkage of
  a weak variance component), so read the scale-axis bootstrap interval as an
  indicator and take the calibrated scale-axis SD interval from `profile_sigma_a`.

```r
library(JuliaCall)
julia_setup()
julia_command('import Pkg; Pkg.activate("/path/to/DRM.jl")')
julia_library("DRM")

julia_assign("y1_r", df$trait1); julia_assign("y2_r", df$trait2)
julia_assign("sp_r", as.integer(df$species_index))
julia_assign("x_r",  df$x)

julia_command('
  phy = augmented_phy(newick_r)                 # or random_balanced_tree for a demo
  dat = (; y1 = y1_r, y2 = y2_r, x = x_r, species = Int.(sp_r))
  form = bf(mu1 = @formula(y1 ~ x + phylo(1 | species)),
            mu2 = @formula(y2 ~ x + phylo(1 | species)),
            sigma1 = @formula(sigma1 ~ 1 + phylo(1 | species)),
            sigma2 = @formula(sigma2 ~ 1 + phylo(1 | species)),
            rho12  = @formula(rho12 ~ 1))
  fit = drm(form, Gaussian(); data = dat, tree = phy)
  pr  = profile_sigma_a(fit; level = 0.90)
  global p_axis = String[String(r.param) for r in pr.summary]
  global p_est  = Float64[r.estimate for r in pr.summary]
  global p_lo   = Float64[r.lower for r in pr.summary]
  global p_hi   = Float64[r.upper for r in pr.summary]
')
data.frame(axis = julia_eval("p_axis"), sd = julia_eval("p_est"),
           lower = julia_eval("p_lo"), upper = julia_eval("p_hi"))
#>        axis     sd lower upper
#> 1    sd_mu1 1.1390 0.885 1.510   # identified, two-sided
#> 2    sd_mu2 0.6478 0.491 0.869
#> 3 sd_sigma1 0.3310 0.193 0.547
#> 4 sd_sigma2 0.0793 0.000 0.234   # collapsed axis: profile lower bound exactly 0
```

The bottom row is the point: `sd_sigma2`'s 90% interval runs to the boundary,
the calibrated statement of "no detectable scale-phylo signal in trait 2's
variance" -- exactly where the native fit reports `pdHess = FALSE` and returns
nothing. For an across-tree analysis, wrap `fit + profile_sigma_a` (and
`bootstrap_sigma_a` for the correlations) in the loop over trees and report, per
quantity, the distribution of the point estimate across trees with the per-tree
profile interval as its uncertainty.
